title: YouTube video The Curse of Evangelion
display_title: YouTube video The Curse of Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Thu Oct 15, 2020 10:48 am I don't know if this is really a worthy thread to make, but I haven't seen anyone talk about this video here before, so I wanted to start a thread for it. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rHIvs0Q-uKI So basically, it's a video called "The Curse of Evangelion," by a user called " " (I'm not kidding, his username is literally a blank space). In a nutshell, it argues that the Rebuild movies were intended to be a critique of the Eva fanbase, and that it uses the character of Rei to do this. He says that the whole sequence at the end of 2.0, where Shinji sacrifices the whole world for Rei, is intended to criticize escapism using the popular character of Rei as a surrogate for all anime waifus, and that the entire movie of 3.0 is intended to punish Shinji (and by extension the audience) for making that decision. It further claims that Kaworu represents reality, and that their relationship will help Shinji return to reality in the final movie. I personally don't agree with the thesis of the video, I think it's overly simplistic and makes Anno seem unnecessarily spiteful against his fans. But I wanted to hear what other people here think. Maybe we can get a worthwhile discussion going.

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by Blockio » Thu Oct 15, 2020 2:35 pm The starting thesis does hold a degree of truth, but the arguments around it are wildly wrong. Especially for Kaworu - he doesn't represent truth, Rebuild Kaworu is the embodyment of escapism, and the very thing that Anno is ciritcising

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Thu Oct 15, 2020 3:36 pm Blockio wrote: The starting thesis does hold a degree of truth, but the arguments around it are wildly wrong. Especially for Kaworu - he doesn't represent truth, Rebuild Kaworu is the embodyment of escapism, and the very thing that Anno is ciritcising

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by Blockio » Thu Oct 15, 2020 3:57 pm I wouldn't call Kaworu a "gender bender Rei" since their character arcs and motivations are rather different, but thematically, they are definitely falling into the same branch; they represent a dependency holding Shinji back, a refusal to grow up and take his life into his own hands

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Oct 16, 2020 1:11 pm In EoE we're told flat out what they represent: "the hope that people can understand each other" and "the words I love you" Neither of these are "escapism" or inherently unrealistic. It might be rare and in-between, but sometimes people do understand each other. Sometimes ppl do love you. To claim that nothing good ever happens would be just as silly as saying that everything's always sunshine & rainbows. Hope is not guarantee; but it's very much human nature. To someone like Shinji who tries to keep expectations low in order to avoid disappointment, they're probably somewhat frightening concepts at times. (indeed Rei's right there with Misato & Asuka in the train scene montage about how the ppl you know may have difficult to deal with sides) They're in fact a big part of why he decides to go back. There's no guarantee that he will have other good experiences - there's a good chance that those two in particular will not be there (Rei less so 'cause she's in the group pic of the people he wants to "see again", if subtly hidden) - but the chance of those positive experiences is why he ultimately risks a world with pain in it over a great big neutral void. In the end neither of them nor any other character is so simple that they could be boiled down to just one singular meaning; but they're not so changed in role & nature that they'd have altogether different meanings in the rebuilds. What happened there is essentially that he hoped he was on the right path, things had gone semi well for him in 2.0... and then that hope was yanked away. I mean see how ReiQ comes in, & how he mistakes her for Rei II at first, hoping that she's still alive... only to find out that that was a futile hope in the end, and it's not just about her as his friend, but about the sense that he accomplished anything worthwhile in his time as a pilot, when it seems that all his efforts were for naught, if not outright counterproductive. In the scene itself there isn't really any mention of escapism; it's about finding a personal reason for fighting. (See the obvious callback to the earlier conversation with Misato, and probably why she cheered him on at first) It's not even when he started having a personal motivation 'cause he always lowkey wanted to get Gendo's attention and for people in general to need him. At most he's admitted it consciously & stopped being focussed on Gendo in particular, who's only given him crumbs, & instead started being focussed on the new connections he made in Tokyo-3. He might as well have been saying that he was doing it for Misato, Asuka or Touji& Kensuke, if they were the ones currently in acute danger. What he's not doing is actively choosing that personal reason over the greater good, 'cause he was given no reason to think that those two things are in conflict. He was deliberately kept ignorant & manipulated. Maybe you could say that he ought to have informed himself more about what the EVAs are & the greater scope situation, but it's not like that would have been an easy feat. In any case this story is not over, so who knows what it's final statement re: personal motivation is going to be; Q is very much the "belly of the whale"/"antithesis" part of the story where everything's called into doubt and question. If anything I got the sense that having no personal reason is what's seen as unrealistic or at least very difficult - there's literally no major character who doesn't have one...

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by Blockio » Fri Oct 16, 2020 2:37 pm Kendrix wrote: In EoE we're told flat out what they represent: "the hope that people can understand each other" and "the words I love you" Neither of these are "escapism" or inherently unrealistic. It might be rare and in-between, but sometimes people do understand each other. Sometimes ppl do love you. To claim that nothing good ever happens would be just as silly as saying that everything's always sunshine & rainbows. Hope is not guarantee; but it's very much human nature. To someone like Shinji who tries to keep expectations low in order to avoid disappointment, they're probably somewhat frightening concepts at times.

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Oct 16, 2020 3:29 pm Blockio wrote: the problem with Rebuild!Kaworu in particular is that he is intentionally withholding information from Shinji to lead him into doing what he thinks should be done; that is not something you do to someone you genuinely care about as a person.

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by BusterMachine4 » Fri Oct 16, 2020 3:40 pm Blockio wrote: Rebuild is a very different ballgame from NGE. What holds true in one does not necessarily have implications for the other. The question on wether or not TV Kaworu perfectly understood TV Shinji aside, the problem with Rebuild!Kaworu in particular is that he is intentionally withholding information from Shinji to lead him into doing what he thinks should be done; that is not something you do to someone you genuinely care about as a person.

[]: Re: YouTube video - "The Curse of Evangelion"             Quote Post by Blockio » Fri Oct 16, 2020 4:36 pm Oh I'm not saying that Shinji is innocent in any way. It's just that if Kaworu was being honest with Shinji about his intentions, there would be no reason for him to be so vague, especially not in regards to the specifics of literally anything Impact related. He at no point specified just how exactly setting off fourth Impact was supposed to reverse third impact (and even about that part he was very vague!), why this being "not our spears" is such bad news, why Rei can't do it, and a bunch of other things that he likely knew the answer to, but chose not to tell Shinji. In addition to all of that, I am working under the assumtion that in the grand scheme of things, Wille have the good of humanity as a whole in mind; them being so violently against what went down in Central Dogma even after Shinji told them that he was "going to fix it" does not exactly instill more trust in NTE!Kaworu's actions. BusterMachine4 wrote: I wouldn’t be so sure about Kaworu not caring for Shinji. From what little we know about his plan with the spears, he wanted to reset the world the way it was, creating a world where they can be happy. All the things he held back were intended to be white lies in pursuit of happiness for the person he cares about. Of course, the plan ended up backfiring horribly, tying back to the anti-escapism message.
